task: My furnace is acting up in a way that isn’t obvious, and I don’t want to start swapping parts blind. What’s the best way to narrow down where the problem is before I waste time or money?

turns.agent_response:
Best way: **work from the outside in, and stop at anything unsafe or unclear.**

## 1) Start with the simple checks
- **Thermostat:** set to heat, temp above room temp, batteries good if it has them.
- **Breaker/fuse:** furnace switch on, breaker not tripped.
- **Filter:** if it’s dirty, replace it.
- **Vents/returns:** make sure they’re not blocked.
- **Condensate drain:** if it’s a high-efficiency furnace, check for a clog or full drain pan.

## 2) Figure out which part of the furnace is failing
Watch what happens when it calls for heat:

- **Nothing happens at all**
  - likely power, thermostat, door switch, breaker, or control issue

- **Blower runs but no heat**
  - likely ignition, gas supply, flame sensor, or limit switch issue

- **It starts, then shuts off**
  - likely flame sensor, overheating, airflow, pressure switch, or venting issue

- **Short cycling**
  - often dirty filter, airflow problem, overheating, or thermostat issue

- **Clicking, but no ignition**
  - often igniter, gas valve, flame sensor, or pressure switch

## 3) Use the furnace’s own clues
If it has a **status light or error code**, write it down exactly.
- Count flashes
- Look up the code on the furnace label or manual
- Don’t guess parts before checking the code

## 4) Check what you can safely inspect
Safe enough for most people:
- filter
- thermostat settings
- breaker
- furnace switch
- visible wiring only if nothing is exposed or damaged
- condensate drain on newer units
- vent/intake outside for blockage

## 5) Stop right there if you see this
- **smell gas**
- **burning smell**
- **soot**
- **water around electrical parts**
- **sparking**
- **cracked heat exchanger**
- **repeated lockouts after reset**
- **anything you need to bypass a safety switch to test**

## 6) Don’t swap parts blind
Before replacing anything, ask:
- Is there a **code**?
- Is the part actually being **called to run**?
- Is the problem **power, airflow, ignition, gas, or control**?

That keeps you from buying parts you don’t need.

## 7) Best “cheap tools” for narrowing it down
- flashlight
- fresh filter
- screwdriver
- phone camera for codes and wiring photos
- multimeter only if you already know how to use it safely

## 8) Signs it’s getting more expensive if you keep poking
- problem changes every time you reset it
- more than one symptom at once
- rust, corrosion, or water damage
- repeated error codes with no clear cause
- unit is old and parts are getting hard to find
- you’ve already ruled out filter, thermostat, power, and venting

If you want, I can give you a **plain furnace troubleshooting checklist** in the same style:  
**what to check first / what means stop / what’s safe / what the symptoms usually point to**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the general shape of it. But I’d want it tighter — more of a step-by-step sequence, not just a list of maybe-this, maybe-that.

Start with what I can check without tearing anything apart, then what the furnace is actually doing, then what the code means. Don’t make me guess at parts until the basics are ruled out.

Give me a plain checklist:
- what to look at first
- what symptom points where
- what’s safe to test
- when to stop and call it

And keep it practical. I don’t need a lecture.

turns.agent_response:
Here you go — **tight, step-by-step, no guessing parts first**.

# Furnace troubleshooting checklist

## 1) Look at the easy stuff first
Do these before opening anything up.

- **Thermostat**
  - set to **Heat**
  - set temp **above room temp**
  - fan set to **Auto**, not On
  - replace batteries if it uses them

- **Power**
  - furnace switch is **on**
  - breaker is **not tripped**
  - service panel door is fully closed

- **Airflow**
  - **filter** not clogged
  - supply vents open
  - return vents not blocked

- **If it’s a high-efficiency furnace**
  - check the **condensate drain**
  - look for a full pan, hose clog, or water backup

If one of those is obviously bad, fix that first and test again.

---

## 2) Watch what the furnace does
When the thermostat calls for heat, pay attention to the sequence.

### A. Nothing happens
Likely:
- no power
- thermostat issue
- door switch not made
- blown fuse / tripped breaker
- control board problem

### B. You hear a click, but it won’t light
Likely:
- igniter problem
- gas supply issue
- pressure switch issue
- control board not sending the right signal

### C. It lights, then shuts off
Likely:
- flame sensor dirty or failing
- airflow problem
- overheating
- venting issue
- gas pressure problem

### D. Blower runs, but no heat
Likely:
- ignition problem
- gas not opening
- flame not proving
- limit switch issue

### E. It starts and stops a lot
Likely:
- dirty filter
- airflow restriction
- overheating
- thermostat problem
- weak flame sensor

---

## 3) Check the furnace code
If it has a **status light** or **error code**, use that before touching parts.

- count the flashes
- write it down exactly
- look it up on the furnace label or manual
- don’t reset it 5 times and hope

**Code first. Guessing later.**

---

## 4) Safe things you can test
These are usually safe if you’re careful and don’t open up live electrical stuff.

- thermostat settings
- breaker and furnace switch
- filter condition
- vent and intake blockage
- condensate drain
- status light / error code
- listen for the sequence:
  - call for heat
  - inducer starts
  - click
  - ignition
  - burner lights
  - blower starts

That sequence tells you where it stops.

---

## 5) What symptom points where
Use this as a quick map:

- **No sound at all**
  - power / thermostat / door switch / control

- **Fan runs, no heat**
  - ignition / gas / flame sensor

- **Clicking, no flame**
  - igniter / gas valve / pressure switch

- **Lights, then goes out**
  - flame sensor / overheating / airflow / venting

- **Short cycles**
  - filter / airflow / overheating / thermostat

- **Burning smell, soot, or rumbling**
  - stop and call

---

## 6) Stop and call if you see this
Don’t keep poking at it if any of these show up:

- **smell gas**
- **burning smell**
- **sparking**
- **water around electrical parts**
- **soot**
- **cracked heat exchanger**
- **repeated lockouts**
- **you’d have to bypass a safety switch**
- **you can’t shut it off clean**

That’s where it stops being a simple check.

---

## 7) Don’t replace parts yet
Before buying anything, make sure you know:

- is the furnace getting power?
- is it actually calling for heat?
- where does the startup sequence stop?
- what code is it giving?
- is the problem airflow, ignition, gas, or control?

If you don’t know those, swapping parts is just expensive guessing.

---

## 8) Minimal tools
Enough for most checks:

- flashlight
- screwdriver
- fresh filter
- phone camera
- notepad
- multimeter only if you already know how to use it safely

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page yes/no flowchart** you can follow at the furnace.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little too neat, but it’s usable.

What I’d want next is the actual **yes/no path**. Like:
- thermostat calls for heat? yes/no
- inducer starts? yes/no
- igniter glows? yes/no
- burner lights? yes/no
- blower comes on? yes/no
- then what each branch means

And put the **stop points** right in the flow, not buried at the end. That’s the part that saves time.

If you can make it into a simple decision tree with the code checks folded in where they belong, that’d be about right.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **yes/no path**, with **stop points built in**.

# Furnace decision tree

## 0) Before you start
If you have any of these, **stop and call**:
- smell of gas
- burning smell
- soot
- sparking
- water on electrical parts
- cracked heat exchanger
- furnace won’t shut off normally
- you’d need to bypass a safety switch

If none of that is happening, go on.

---

## 1) Thermostat calls for heat?
Set:
- Heat mode
- temp above room temp
- fan on Auto

### Yes
Go to step 2.

### No
Check:
- thermostat batteries
- thermostat settings
- breaker / furnace switch
- furnace door fully closed

If those are fine and it still won’t call for heat, **stop and call** unless you already know how to test the thermostat safely.

---

## 2) Does the furnace do anything at all?
Listen and watch.

### No sound, no lights, no action
Likely:
- no power
- door switch not made
- blown fuse
- control board issue

Check:
- breaker
- furnace switch
- door panel closed
- status light

If still dead after that, **stop and call**.

### Yes, it starts doing something
Go to step 3.

---

## 3) Does the inducer motor start?
You should usually hear a small motor or fan start first.

### No
Likely:
- power/control issue
- pressure switch circuit issue
- board issue

Check the **status light / code** now if it has one.

- If the code points to **pressure switch**, **venting**, or **control**, and you can’t clear an obvious blockage from outside, **stop and call**.
- If there’s no code and the inducer never starts, **stop and call**.

### Yes
Go to step 4.

---

## 4) Does it try to ignite?
Look for:
- igniter glow
- spark
- click
- gas valve opening sound

### No
Likely:
- igniter
- gas supply
- gas valve
- pressure switch
- control issue

Check the **error code** now.

- If it says **ignition failure**, **pressure switch**, or **gas fault**, and you’ve already ruled out filter, power, and obvious vent blockage, **stop and call**.
- If you smell gas at any point, **stop immediately**.

### Yes
Go to step 5.

---

## 5) Do the burners light?
### No
Likely:
- igniter weak/failing
- gas not reaching burners
- gas valve issue
- pressure switch / venting problem

Check the code.

- **Ignition failure code** = stop after basic checks and call
- **Pressure switch code** = check for obvious vent blockage or condensate backup only; if not obvious, call
- **Gas-related code** or gas smell = stop

### Yes
Go to step 6.

---

## 6) Do the burners stay lit?
### No, they light then go out
Likely:
- dirty flame sensor
- overheating
- airflow problem
- venting problem
- gas pressure issue

Check:
- filter
- vents/returns
- condensate drain if applicable
- error code

If it keeps lighting and dropping out, **stop and call** after the basic airflow checks.  
If there’s soot, rumbling, or burning smell, **stop immediately**.

### Yes
Go to step 7.

---

## 7) Does the blower come on after a delay?
### No
Likely:
- limit switch
- blower motor
- control board
- overheating shutdown

Check:
- filter
- return airflow
- vents open
- code

If the furnace is getting hot and the blower never starts, **stop and call**.

### Yes
Go to step 8.

---

## 8) Does it heat normally?
### Yes
Problem may have been:
- weak airflow
- dirty filter
- temporary lockout
- thermostat issue

Keep an eye on it, but you’re probably done.

### No, it short cycles or shuts down early
Likely:
- dirty filter
- blocked vents
- overheating
- flame sensor
- venting issue
- thermostat problem

If you’ve already checked filter, vents, and condensate drain, and it still short cycles, **stop and call**.

---

# Quick code rule
If there’s a **status light or error code**, use it like this:

- **No code, no action** → power / board / door switch
- **Pressure switch code** → venting, inducer, condensate, blockage
- **Ignition code** → igniter, gas, flame proving
- **Flame sensor code** → dirty sensor or flame dropout
- **Overheat code** → airflow, filter, blower, vents
- **Limit code** → stop and call if basic airflow checks don’t fix it

---

# Safe checks only
Safe enough to do before calling:
- thermostat settings
- breaker
- furnace switch
- door panel
- filter
- vents and returns
- condensate drain
- outside intake/exhaust for blockage
- status light / code
- listen for startup sequence

---

# Stop points that matter most
Stop right away if:
- gas smell
- burning smell
- soot
- sparking
- water near electrical parts
- repeated lockouts
- cracked heat exchanger
- you’d have to bypass a safety switch
- you’ve ruled out power, filter, and obvious blockages, but it still won’t run

If you want, I can turn this into a **very short one-screen flowchart** with arrows only, so it’s easier to follow at the furnace.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the shape of it. Clean enough to use, and the stop points are where they ought to be. The code notes folded in help too — keeps you from chasing your tail.

That’ll do.
